$comment_provenance: L-F3 STARTER ORACLE — 20 hand-curated ADF→Python expression pairs. Each `expected_python` is derived from a human reading of the ADF expression spec, NOT from running wkmigrate's emitter. This is the first independent oracle for X-2 (semantic_equivalence) measurements; the existing `golden_sets/expressions.json` was generated by running wkmigrate's own emitter and is therefore wkmigrate-circular (it can detect regressions but cannot falsify the emitter's notion of correctness). See dev/autodev-sessions/LMV-AUTODEV-2026-04-08-session2.md L-F3 for the full rationale.
$comment_methodology: Each pair was hand-written with these criteria: (1) the ADF expression is unambiguous per the ADF expression-language docs, (2) the `expected_python` is the most natural Python translation a human would write for the same semantics, (3) the pair probes a specific axis (function arity, nesting, type coercion, ternary, dispatch). When future X-2 measurements compare wkmigrate's emitter output against these `expected_python` values and they disagree, the disagreement is a real signal — wkmigrate either has a bug, or our human-derived expected is wrong, or there's a semantic gap worth filing as a finding. (Trust-but-verify: whichever side is wrong gets fixed.)
$comment_schema: Same shape as golden_sets/expressions.json (count, expressions[]) so it can be loaded with the same code path. The only difference is the source of expected_python.
$comment_categories: category labels match expressions.json: string, math, datetime, logical, collection, nested. The `axis` field is L-F3-specific and tags the specific edge case being probed.
$comment_referenced_params: Pairs that reference @pipeline().parameters.X include a `referenced_params` array of {name, type} dicts. The activity_context_wrapper helpers (wrap_in_set_variable, wrap_in_if_condition, etc.) inject these into the synthetic pipeline's `parameters` block so wkmigrate's parameter resolver can find them. Type strings follow ADF conventions (String, Int, Float, Bool). Pairs without referenced_params are pure literal expressions and need no injection — the field is optional and absent on the original L-F3 starter pairs.
$comment_w_targets: Pairs added in the L-F3 corpus growth (PR follow-up to L-F19) carry a `targets` array of W-finding IDs from dev/wkmigrate-issue-map.json. This lets sweep tooling cross-reference each adversarial pair with the failure signatures it is meant to exercise. Examples: targets=['W-2'] for parameter refs in non-notebook contexts; targets=['W-3'] for math on parameters; targets=['W-10'] for bare ForEach arrays.

expressions.rationale: ADF concat() takes 2-N args. Tests whether wkmigrate flattens the call into a chain of str()+str() rather than collapsing to a single literal.
ADF replace(s, old, new). Natural Python is the str.replace method, not re.sub or string concat.
Nested string method calls. Tests whether wkmigrate's emitter preserves call order (lower then upper) vs accidentally collapsing.
ADF trim() = Python str.strip() (both strip leading + trailing whitespace; ADF doesn't have separate ltrim/rtrim built-ins).
Tests whether wkmigrate emits explicit parentheses around the inner mul (preserving evaluation order even though * has higher precedence than +).

AMBIGUOUS: ADF div(int, int) might be integer division (3) or float division (3.333...). Python `/` is float division; `//` is integer. If wkmigrate emits `//` and we expected `/`, that's a real semantic disagreement worth investigating.
ADF mod() = Python %. Tests basic operator dispatch.
Tests boolean operator translation AND parenthesisation of operands.
Tests greater/less → > / <. ADF has greaterOrEquals/lessOrEquals that should map to >= / <=.
Tests whether wkmigrate emits Python `not` keyword vs `!` (which is not valid Python).
ADF int() casts a string to integer. Direct Python int() call.
ADF string() casts a value to string. Direct Python str() call.
ADF float() casts a string to float. Direct Python float() call.
ADF length() of array = Python len(). createArray() = Python list literal.
ADF first() returns first element. Python idiom is subscript [0]. AMBIGUOUS: wkmigrate might emit list[0] or next(iter(list)).
ADF last() returns last element. Python idiom is [-1].

ADF formatDateTime uses .NET format patterns (yyyy-MM-dd). Python uses strftime patterns (%Y-%m-%d). Tests whether wkmigrate translates the pattern AND uses datetime.fromisoformat / strftime vs a custom helper. wkmigrate currently emits _wkmigrate_format_datetime() which is NOT what a human would naturally write — this pair is the canonical L-F3 'oracle disagreement' candidate.
ADF addDays returns a date string. Python natural translation uses datetime.timedelta. wkmigrate may use a custom helper instead — another expected L-F3 disagreement.
Composition: concat(str_literal, string(add(1, 2))). Tests whether wkmigrate handles 3-deep nesting without dropping any layer.
ADF if(cond, then, else) = Python ternary expression. Tests both the ternary translation AND the nested mod-equals predicate.

Bare @createArray(...) is the canonical ForEach.items input. The W-10 corrected diagnosis showed that the original 33 collection-category entries in expressions.json all wrap createArray inside a scalar function (length, first, last, empty), so the bare-array path was never exercised. This pair fills that gap so the sweep can prove ForEach with createArray actually resolves on alpha_1.

Same as the string variant — exercises bare integer-array ForEach items. Two variants instead of one because wkmigrate's _parse_array_string in for_each_activity_translator strips quotes uniformly, so int and string handling could diverge silently if only one is tested.
Singleton-array edge case. The _parse_array_string regex splits on commas; a singleton tests that the no-comma path doesn't degenerate.

Bare @pipeline().parameters.X reference. wkmigrate maps this to dbutils.widgets.get(name). W-2 says this should work in non-notebook contexts (SetVariable, IfCondition, Copy); the existing 200-pair corpus uses literal values only and never references parameters, so the resolution path is not exercised. This pair flows through every activity context via sweep-activity-contexts.

Parameter ref nested inside a string function call. Tests that wkmigrate's resolver walks into the function args, finds the parameter ref, and emits dbutils.widgets.get(name) inline (not as a temp variable).

Parameter ref inside an IfCondition predicate. The IfCondition activity context is where W-2 has historically been weakest because the predicate is decomposed into op/left/right at IR construction time and the operand resolution must happen inside that decomposition. Probes the L-F17 IfCondition synthesis path AND any wkmigrate-side IR op-name → Python operator mapping.

Math on a parameter declared as Int. The W-3 fix should use the declared type to wrap the dbutils.widgets.get call with int(). wkmigrate's emitter on alpha_1 actually uses a defensive runtime lambda — if it disagrees with this human-written expected, that's an L-F3 oracle disagreement worth investigating (the static int() wrap is more readable and could be chosen if the parameter type is statically known).

Math over two typed parameter operands — Int qty and Float price. Tests that the type coercion path picks the correct wrapper per parameter, not a single uniform cast. If wkmigrate emits float() for both (or int() for both), the type info from the declaration was lost.
